steps.reveal: Pater = Father! (voc., direct address in prayer)

hints.nominative: Nominative would also be 'Pater,' but here it is direct address — vocative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'patris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'patris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'patris' ends in -is

hints.to be strong: 'Vis' as a noun means force — but here 'vis' is the verb 'velle' (to wish): 2nd sg. pres. indic.

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'velis' — 'vis' is the irregular present indicative 2nd sg.
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'voluisti' — 'vis' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'voles' — 'vis' is present indicative

hints.valeo, valere, valui, valitum: These are the parts of 'valere' (to be strong/well)
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live)

hints.to transform: 'To transform' is 'transformare' — 'transferre' means to carry across or remove
hints.to translate: While 'translatum' gives us 'translate,' the base meaning is to carry across / remove
hints.to transfer ownership: That is an extended legal meaning — the core meaning is to carry across or remove

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd sg. would be 'transfers' — 'transfer' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'transferas' — 'transfer' is imperative
hints.infinitive: The infinitive is 'transferre' — 'transfer' is imperative singular

hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry) — the base verb without trans-
hints.affero, afferre, attuli, allatum: These are the parts of 'afferre' (to bring to) — different prefix
hints.refero, referre, rettuli, relatum: These are the parts of 'referre' (to carry back) — different prefix

steps.reveal: calicem istum = this cup (acc., object of 'transfer')

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'calix iste' — 'calicem istum' with -em/-um is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'calice isto' — 'calicem istum' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'calicis istius' — 'calicem istum' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'calicis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'calicis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'calicis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What does 'calicem' metaphorically represent in this context?
steps.target: the suffering and death Jesus is about to undergo
choices: the suffering and death Jesus is about to undergo
the Eucharistic cup at the Last Supper
the cup of God's wrath against Israel

hints.the Eucharistic cup at the Last Supper: The Eucharistic cup was already shared — this 'calix' in Gethsemane represents the coming Passion
hints.a literal cup of wine: The cup is metaphorical — it represents suffering, not a physical vessel
hints.the cup of God's wrath against Israel: While OT prophetic imagery informs it, here the 'cup' is specifically Jesus's suffering

steps.prompt: What type of conditional clause is 'si vis'?

hints.contrary-to-fact (present unreal): Contrary-to-fact would use imperfect subjunctive ('si velles') — 'vis' is present indicative
hints.future less vivid: Future less vivid would use present subjunctive ('si velis') — 'vis' is indicative
hints.mixed conditional: A mixed conditional has different tenses in protasis and apodosis — this is straightforward present real

translation: Father, if you wish, remove this cup from me
